title: The pogroms after the Holocaust
gist: The 1946 Kielce pogrom, triggered by a child's fabricated abduction story invoking the ancient 'blood libel' myth, drove up to 300,000 Jews to flee postwar eastern Europe; the same blood-libel myth and a comparable spike in antisemitic incidents and emigration intent have resurfaced across Europe since October 2023, per EU and US tracking data.

insights.claim: The July 1946 Kielce pogrom was sparked entirely by a fabricated story from an 8-year-old boy, Henryk Błaszczyk, who invented a tale of Jewish abduction to avoid punishment for running away from home, showing how a single unverified false claim catalyzed one of postwar Europe's deadliest antisemitic massacres.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a single unverified rumor, absent institutional skepticism, can trigger mass violence.
insights.locator: The story begins with an eight-year-old boy

insights.claim: Polish militia investigating the boy's claim found no basement and no abducted children at 7 Planty Street within about an hour, yet by then rumors of a Jewish ritual 'blood libel' murder had already spread through the town and a mob had formed.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Debunking a rumor at its source does not stop it once it has propagated socially.
insights.locator: When, an hour later, the militia finally arrived

insights.claim: The Kielce massacre was carried out by an integrated cross-section of the community — soldiers, policemen, militiamen, factory workers and civilian men and women — including the very state security forces nominally sent to restore order, who instead joined in beating and killing victims.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Shows how mass violence can be enabled, not restrained, by the institutions meant to prevent it.
insights.locator: The striking thing about this massacre

insights.claim: According to Warsaw's Polin Museum, at least 40 Jews were killed and roughly 80 injured at Kielce, including three decorated Jewish army veterans and two non-Jewish Poles mistaken for Jews, plus a pregnant woman and a mother killed alongside her newborn.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Establishes the documented scale and indiscriminate nature of the massacre.
insights.locator: According to the Polin Museum in Warsaw

themes.theme: Blood libel as a recurring, mutating myth

insights.claim: The author's core thesis is that the ancient antisemitic 'blood libel' myth was the proximate trigger not just at Kielce but also at Rzeszów, Kraków (August 1945), and multiple Hungarian towns in 1946, with the specific narrative detail (blood used for reviving survivors, for sausages) mutating across locations while the underlying structure stayed constant.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Identifies a stable, recognizable narrative pattern that recurs across otherwise unconnected outbreaks of antisemitic violence.
insights.locator: The Kielce pogrom was the most violent instance

insights.claim: The same blood libel myth resurfaced in March 2026 near the White House, where a street performance depicted Trump, Netanyahu and Epstein tearing apart an infant and drinking its blood, which the author explicitly frames as a 'deliberate invocation' of the same calumny that drove Kielce 80 years earlier.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Shows the same centuries-old antisemitic trope resurfacing verbatim in contemporary Western political theater.
insights.locator: a group of activists staged a street performance

themes.theme: Postwar Jewish flight and demographic consequence

insights.claim: Historian Yehuda Bauer estimates 90,000-95,000 Jews fled eastern Europe in July-September 1946 alone, immediately following Kielce, out of roughly 300,000 total who fled antisemitic persecution in the five years after WWII, a figure the author calls 'if anything, a slightly conservative estimate.'
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the postwar flight directly triggered by pogrom violence.
insights.locator: According to Holocaust historian Yehuda Bauer

insights.claim: The author's striking claim is that even Hitler's Holocaust had not fully emptied Poland and eastern Europe of Jews, but postwar local pogrom-driven flight in 1945-46 completed the 'Judenfrei' outcome the Nazis themselves had failed to achieve.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Reframes postwar civilian violence as functionally completing a genocidal demographic goal the war itself did not finish.
insights.quote: The great irony is that even Hitler had not managed to make these regions 'Judenfrei': in many areas that task was only finally completed by local people in the years after the Holocaust was supposed to be over.
insights.locator: The great irony is that even Hitler

themes.theme: Present-day antisemitism resurgence, by the numbers

insights.claim: Per the EU's Fundamental Rights Agency, antisemitic incidents in Poland increased eight-fold after Israel went to war in Gaza, and violent anti-Jewish attacks rose across Europe in the months following 7 October 2023.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: Provides a quantified baseline for tracking how Middle East conflict flashpoints translate into European antisemitic incident rates.
insights.locator: According to the EU's Agency for Fundamental Rights

insights.claim: The US-based Combat Anti-Semitism Movement recorded a 34% increase in global antisemitism in a single week in March 2026, coinciding with the Israel-US war against Iran, indicating antisemitic incident rates track geopolitical Israel-related flashpoints in near real time.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: A fast-moving leading indicator linking regional conflict escalation to antisemitic incident spikes.
insights.locator: According to the US-based Combat Anti-Semitism Movement

insights.claim: In the four months preceding publication, bombs were set off at Jewish schools, restaurants and synagogues in Liège, Rotterdam, Amsterdam and Munich, and London saw arson attacks followed by the stabbing of two Jewish men in late April.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated pattern of escalating violent incidents across multiple Western European cities.
insights.locator: In the past four months, makeshift bombs

insights.claim: The FRA's most recent survey found 45% of European Jews have considered emigrating, rising above 50% in Germany specifically, meaning safety-driven emigration intent is now a majority-adjacent sentiment among German Jews.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: A leading indicator of accelerating Jewish demographic flight from parts of Europe.
insights.locator: In the most recent survey of Jewish people in Europe

insights.claim: British Jewish emigration to Israel doubled in the year following the 7 October 2023 Hamas attacks, and a subsequent survey found only a third of British Jews believe there is a long-term future for Jews in Britain, with half having considered leaving.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: A UK-specific confidence collapse despite Britain's comparatively stronger rule-of-law institutions.
insights.locator: Despite the war in Gaza, Jewish emigration from Britain

insights.claim: The author notes that in the 80 years since Kielce, Europe has not seen any antisemitic attack on that scale, crediting more robust law-and-order institutions, but frames current incident and emigration data as evidence that 'violence lurks just beneath the surface' even in a much more institutionally stable Europe.
insights.speaker: Keith Lowe
insights.so_what: A nuanced middle-ground read: stronger institutions have raised the threshold for mass violence, but have not eliminated the underlying risk.
insights.quote: But even now, violence lurks just beneath the surface.
insights.locator: We live in a world today where law and order
